cases.title: Three Cities, One Rash
cases.presentation: Over 24 hours, EDs in three cities several hundred miles apart each report several previously healthy adults with high fever and a progressing vesicular rash that started on the face and hands. None have travel links to each other.
cases.question: What should a clinician who recognizes this do?
options: A: Treat each case locally and wait for lab confirmation before contacting anyone.
B: Immediately notify public health of a possible smallpox/high-consequence event, isolate patients with airborne + contact precautions, and protect staff.
C: Discharge the patients to reduce ED crowding.
D: Assume it is chickenpox and take no special action.
cases.explanation: Simultaneous multi-site clusters of an unusual, severe illness are a classic epidemiologic clue. A centrifugal vesicular rash raises smallpox concern — an airborne, high-consequence Category A agent. Early notification, airborne + contact isolation, and staff protection are the pivotal actions; waiting for confirmation squanders the response window.
cases.clinicalPearl: Three separated clusters at once is the population's way of shouting 'point source' — report before you confirm.

cases.title: Powder in the Mailroom
cases.presentation: An office reports an envelope leaking white powder; the mailroom worker who opened it is well but frightened. Public health later confirms Bacillus anthracis. Twelve coworkers shared the space.
cases.question: What is the appropriate management of the exposed but well coworkers?
options: A: Isolate them as if they were infectious to others.
B: Post-exposure prophylaxis (antibiotics ± vaccination) through public health, with monitoring — anthrax is not spread person-to-person.
C: No action unless they become ill.
D: Immediate ICU admission for all.
cases.explanation: Inhalational anthrax is not transmitted person-to-person, so isolating exposed-but-well contacts is unnecessary; the priority is timely post-exposure prophylaxis (antibiotics, possibly vaccine) dispensed via public health, with monitoring. Prophylaxis is time-sensitive.
cases.clinicalPearl: Anthrax spreads from the envelope, not from the patient — the intervention for the exposed is prophylaxis, not isolation.

cases.title: Month Two of the Pandemic
cases.presentation: Two months into a respiratory pandemic, your hospital faces N95 shortages, one-third of staff out sick or caring for family, and ICU demand exceeding ventilator supply. Administrators ask how to sustain the response.
cases.question: Which combination of principles applies?
options: A: Abandon PPE and treat everyone in the ICU regardless of resources.

B: Protect and conserve the workforce (PPE conservation/extended use, vaccination, mental-health support), expand surge capacity and continuity plans, and prepare crisis standards of care for resource allocation.
C: Close the hospital until supplies arrive.
D: Ignore staffing and focus only on beds.
cases.explanation: Prolonged events are won by sustaining the workforce and the system: PPE conservation and vaccination protect staff, surge and continuity plans expand and preserve essential services, and crisis standards of care provide an ethical/legal framework for allocating scarce resources like ventilators.
cases.clinicalPearl: In a pandemic the scarce resource is often the staff — protect the people and the beds follow.
